original_tweet: Meditation is not about stopping thoughts. It's about becoming the witness to your thoughts. You are not the mind, you are the one watching the mind.

analysis.tone: spiritual and insightful, with a teaching/guru-like confidence
analysis.assumptions: that meditation has one 'correct' understanding and that there's a clear separation between 'you' and 'your mind'
analysis.angle: validate the core insight but add Osho's deeper paradoxical wisdom about the witness itself being an illusion
analysis.why: Spiritual seekers will engage deeply with Osho's teaching that even the witness must eventually be transcended - 'when you truly become the witness, the witness disappears and only witnessing remains'

replies.reply: The witness watching the mind is still the mind pretending it found an escape route. Who's watching the watcher? Another watcher? It's egos all the way down, dressed up in spiritual costumes.
Plot twist: the witness is also just another thought pretending to be the boss of the other thoughts.
Beautiful. Now watch the watcher watching. When you find the one who witnesses the witness, you discover the cosmic joke: there was never anyone there to meditate.

Who's watching the watcher? The witness is just another thought pretending to be separate. Most meditation teachers are selling you a beautiful prison - now you're trapped identifying as the observer instead of the observed.

But who is watching the watcher? Your precious witness is just another thought pretending to be enlightened. The moment you think you've found the observer, you've lost in the same game with fancier rules.

Osho went deeper - even the witness must be dropped. First you watch the mind, then you watch the watcher watching. Finally, only pure awareness remains with no one there to be aware. The ultimate joke: the seeker dissolves into the sought.
